content:
The same technology that is scanning your codebase for security holes is the reason those holes exist in the first place.

7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Kevin Horvat on UnsplashIn April 2026 Broadcom announced what it described as the largest set of Spring security updates in the framework’s 23-year history.
The number that should make every backend engineer stop and read carefully is this one. Monthly security advisories reported to Broadcom by the Spring community jumped more than 1,700% from March to April 2026. Not a gradual increase. Not a concerning trend. A 1,700% spike in a single month.
Spring runs in more than half of Fortune 500 companies. It is the backbone of enterprise Java development globally. When something changes dramatically in Spring’s security posture it is not a niche problem. It is a problem affecting an enormous portion of production infrastructure that real businesses depend on.
What changed is not that Spring suddenly became insecure. What changed is AI.
Two things happened simultaneously
To understand the 1,700% spike you need to understand that two completely separate AI-driven phenomena…
